title_en: Hadiths 21–30: Purification of the Soul and Ethical Principles
content_en:
The third group of hadiths in al-Arba'een deepens the exploration of personal ethics, the purification of the soul, and the comprehensive moral framework that Islam provides for human conduct. These hadiths are among those most frequently cited by scholars of Islamic spirituality and character development.

Hadith 21 — 'Say: I believe in Allah, then be steadfast' — is a remarkable compression of the entire Islamic moral project: declare your faith and then live consistently with it, without vacillation or compromise. Ibn Rajab's commentary on this hadith traces the concept of istiqamah (steadfastness) through the Quranic references and the sayings of the Companions, arguing that Umar ibn al-Khattab identified it as the most comprehensive single principle of the religion.

Hadith 22 on the prohibition of squandering property and the obligation to preserve it, and Hadith 23 — 'Purification is half of faith; saying Alhamdulillah fills the scale; SubhanAllah and Alhamdulillah fill what is between the heavens and the earth; prayer is a light; charity is a proof; patience is an illumination; and the Quran is a proof for you or against you' — are both foundational statements that Ibn Rajab treats as summaries of the experiential dimension of Islam.

Hadith 25 — 'Charity is due upon every joint of a person every day the sun rises: judging justly between two people is charity; assisting a man with his mount is charity; a good word is charity; every step you take towards the prayer is charity; and removing a harmful thing from the road is charity' — expands the understanding of sadaqah beyond financial giving to encompass the full range of beneficial action in daily life. Hadiths 27 and 29 on righteousness and the avoidance of wrongdoing establish the comprehensive Quranic principle: 'Righteousness is good character, and wrongdoing is what pricks your conscience and you hate for others to know about.'
